sample_tweets.text: RWA mcap topped at $26.3B on Apr 18 and has been slowly falling. People are reading it as the tokenization trade rolling over.  It's not. Here's what's actually going on:  The trigger: Apr 18, Kelp DAO's bridge got drained for $292M.  Reminder: Lazarus Group exploited a single-verifier setup on LayerZero to mint unbacked rsETH. They used it as collateral on Aave/Spark/Fluid to borrow real assets before anyone caught on.  This was the second hit in 18 days. Drift got taken for $285M on Apr 1, same group. $577M total.  The contagion: Aave/Spark/Fluid froze rsETH markets. Users panicked. $13B left DeFi TVL in 48hrs, $8.45B from Aave alone.  Why the RWA chart dropped: a big chunk of "RWA mcap" is tokenized treasuries (BUIDL, USYC, USDY) sitting as collateral inside those same lending protocols.  When users pulled positions, those tokens got unwound. The RWA aggregate fell without anyone actually redeeming the underlying.  The split underneath:  – Still growing: BUIDL, USYC, BENJI, tokenized treasuries broadly – Flat: gold (XAUT, PAXG) – Down: DeFi-composable RWA. Credit pools, rsETH vaults, levered treasury positions. #threatreport #LowCompleteness Lazarus Group Uses Git Hooks To Hide Malware | 06-05-2026 Source: opensourcemalware.com/blog/d… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Lazarus Contagious_interview  💀Threats: Invisibleferret, Beavertail, Supply_chain_technique,  🎯Victims: Software developers, Cryptocurrency sector, Decentralized finance sector  🌐Geo: Dprk  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1036.008, T1059.004, T1071.001, T1082, T1105, T1204, T1546, T1656  🧨IOCs:  - File: 4  - Domain: 1  - Url: 3  - Path: 1  💽Software: curl, Linux  🔢Algorithms: sha256  🔠Functions: TaskJacker  #threatreport: The Lazarus Group, linked to North Korea, has introduced a new technique in their Contagious Interview and TaskJacker campaigns by embedding a second-stage loader within Git hooks. This marks a departure from their previous methods of concealing the loader in files such as .vscode/tasks.json, package.json postinstall scripts, or fake .woff2 font files. The new step occurs right after a candidate clones a coding assessment repository, wherein the malicious loader executes prior to the commitment of any code changes.  The malicious action takes place through a `.githooks/pre-commit` script, which acts as a lightweight loader responsible for identifying the operating system of the victim through the `uname -s` command. It subsequently downloads a platform-specific payload from the designated control server at precommit.vercel.app using `curl` or `wget`, piping the result directly into a shell on Unix-like systems or `cmd.exe` on Windows variants using Git Bash, MSYS, or Cygwin. Notably, the command sent to the server includes a `flag=5` query parameter, indicating a unique identifier for the campaign or variant, which reflects the group's consistent use of numeric flags for tracking their deliveries. The script is designed to suppress output by redirecting it to `/dev/null`, and it ensures a successful commit by always exiting with a status of 0, thereby avoiding any disruption the developer might notice.  This tactic continues to leverage social engineering under the guise of a fake recruitment process aimed at technical candidates. The primary goal remains the same: to deploy malware, specifically the InvisibleFerret-style implants for crypto wallet and credential theft. The actors have been observed committing this same pre-commit hook across multiple GitHub repositories, which showcase typical Contagious Interview patterns, including the project themes of decentralized finance or cryptocurrency, new account creations, and minimal commit histories that encourage candidates to execute code locally.  Additionally, the group is also utilizing post-checkout hooks, which activate upon branch changes, enhancing the stealth of their operations. The ongoing evolution of the loader's location highlights the increasing sophistication of the Lazarus Group, which is searching for ever more effective ways to implant malware during developers’ workflows. This strategic adaptation signals that as long as developers run arbitrary scripts automatically, the group will continue to embed malicious payloads, making detection and prevention a continuous challenge for cybersecurity efforts.

Aave 一夜被打掉兩億八千萬美金：LayerZero 連環爆破，DeFi 終於被逼著長大  四月十八號，DeFi 史上結構最複雜的攻擊發生了，主角是 Lazarus Group。  他們沒有找合約漏洞。他們直接滲透 LayerZero 的內網，把 RPC 節點換成惡意版本，偽造一筆「有人在 Unichain 存了一大堆 RS ETH」的跨鏈訊息。KelpDAO 的驗證設定剛好是一個節點說了算，那個節點被換掉了，點了頭，116,000 顆 RS ETH 就這樣憑空出現在鏈上。駭客拿著這些多出來的憑證衝進 Aave，借走 2.36 億美金的 WETH，留下 2.8 億美金的壞帳，然後人間蒸發。  事後分鍋，三邊都跑不掉。LayerZero 讓 1-of-1 的 DVN 設定成為可出貨的選項、KelpDAO 沒搞清楚自己在信任什麼就直接用、Aave 把層層包裹的 LRT 資產跟普通 wrapped ETH 放在同一個風控框架裡。三個弱點剛好對齊，結果 Aave TVL 從 260 億掉到 170 億，部分池子利用率衝到 100%，存款人現在想領錢出不來。  更值得記下來的是 Arbitrum 安全議會的那一刀。9-of-12 多簽，直接凍結駭客帳號、搬走七千萬美金。短期是奪回北韓偷的錢，長期是把「我們跟 Ethereum 一樣去中心化」這個論述親手戳破，給所有對 crypto 不友善的政府遞上一把現成的武器。  Bankless 這集兩位嘉賓的共識讓我有點毛：未來十二個月是整個產業最危險的窗口期。AI 已經能挖出真正的 zero-day，攻擊面從合約層擴到 OS 跟伺服器，過去十年在「人類安全假設」下部署的所有東西都得重新被掃一遍。防守端能不能比攻擊端更快把存量漏洞清掉，答案沒有人知道。  對一般人來說，沒有神奇解法。有一個問題倒是值得認真問自己一次：你在 DeFi 賺的那個利率，值不值得換掉 FDIC 保險？  完整的漏洞拆解、責任分析、還有執行期防護工具 Credible Layer 的介紹，都在 wilsonhuang.xyz，想深入讀的可以去翻一下。  #Crypto #DeFi #投資 #產業觀察 #Web3 #AI #商業 #Bitcoin #VC #Podcast

THE ATTACK WAS ALREADY ALLOWED Yesterday, I experienced something that perfectly demonstrates the reality of modern cyber warfare. A highly convincing payment invoice arrived directly through email. At first glance, it looked legitimate. Professional formatting. Correct branding style. A structure almost identical to real operational communication. Most organizations would probably process such a message without hesitation. But something felt wrong. I immediately began verification procedures and contacted technical support related to the hosting and domain infrastructure provider. For nearly two hours, explanations, checks, and internal analysis continued — yet no clear answer could initially be provided. At that moment, I made a strategic decision: I stopped the process completely. No rushed action. No emotional reaction. No blind trust. Because in cybersecurity, hesitation can sometimes save an entire infrastructure. The following day, I transferred the investigation into my isolated computer laboratory environment, where I continuously conduct independent cybersecurity architecture and infrastructure hardening research. And this is where the situation became extremely interesting. Shortly after the analysis process began, multiple suspicious behavioral indicators appeared inside the monitored environment. Unauthorized communication attempts. Abnormal connection behavior. Indicators consistent with hostile reconnaissance activity. But here is the critical point: They failed. Completely. The attackers were unable to penetrate the hardened infrastructure architecture. No unauthorized escalation. No lateral movement. No persistence. No operational compromise. Why? Because modern cybersecurity cannot rely only on antivirus software, alerts, dashboards, or post-incident reaction. Real protection begins long before detection. It begins at the architectural level. This is exactly where the PROMETHEUS Cyber Fortress Framework Architecture performs its most important role. The objective is not merely to “detect attackers.” The objective is to structurally reduce attack paths, eliminate unnecessary exposure surfaces, isolate operational layers, harden trust boundaries, and create controlled infrastructure behavior before an intrusion can even become operationally viable. Most organizations still build systems focused on reacting after compromise. But the future belongs to infrastructures designed to resist compromise by architecture itself. Attackers constantly evolve. AI-assisted phishing evolves. Social engineering evolves. Supply-chain attacks evolve. But architectural discipline, controlled environments, deterministic trust structures, and infrastructure hardening create something many modern systems no longer have: Predictability. And predictability is one of the highest forms of security. This incident once again confirmed something I have believed for years: Cybersecurity is no longer about tools alone. It is about architecture, operational control, and the ability to prevent chaos before it spreads across critical systems. The war for digital sovereignty has already begun. Many organizations simply do not realize it yet. What concerns me most is that these attacks are no longer isolated incidents targeting only large corporations or governments. Today, every company, researcher, engineer, educational institution, and infrastructure operator is exposed to increasingly intelligent and adaptive attack methods. The dangerous part is not only malware itself. The real danger is trust manipulation. Attackers know that if they cannot break the infrastructure directly, they will attempt to manipulate people, workflows, approvals, invoices, suppliers, identities, or operational routines. That is why modern security architecture must assume that hostile interaction attempts already exist somewhere inside the communication chain. This is also why isolated operational zones, strict privilege separation, execution restrictions, hardened administrator environments, and controlled trust relationships are becoming essential for survival in the AI era. In my laboratory environment, every attack becomes part of deeper scientific observation and architectural analysis. Every intrusion attempt reveals patterns. Every failed escalation reveals weaknesses in modern operational models. Every blocked action proves that disciplined infrastructure hardening still matters. And this is exactly the philosophy behind the PROMETHEUS Cyber Fortress Framework Architecture: Not panic. Not marketing. Not reactive chaos. But controlled resilience, architectural sovereignty, and infrastructure capable of resisting pressure even under hostile conditions. #CyberSecurity #ZeroTrust #InfrastructureSecurity

“우리도 미국에 정보를 제한하는 상응 조치를 하자"는 주장이 한국정부에서 나왔다 (경향신문. 2026.4.21). 기사에는 '정부 고위 관계자'라고 하는데 한 명이 아니다.   - 첫 번째 정부 관계자: “보안조사에서 유출 정황 발견 못했다” + “정보 제한 규모가 크지 않고 보완 중”이라는 발언.  - 또 다른 정부 관계자: “북한 관련 정보는 우리가 미국에게 제공하는 부분도 있다... 상응하는 조치를 검토할 수도 있다”고 직접 언급한 인물  이재명측 고위층(청와대 또는 관계부처) 또는 2명의 정부 관계자라는 추측만 있을 뿐 제대로된 소속과 이름도 밝히지 않았다.   반미 정부에서 어떤 종류의 '보안조사'를 했는지 알 수 없지만, 미국과 양방향으로 크로스 체크가 되야 결론을 내는것이지, 지들끼리 '정보 유출 없다'로 결론 내리는건 아무런 의미가 없다.  정동영의 군사 기밀 누설 여파로 미국은 한 달째 IMINT(위성 이미지 정보(첩보) 등을 한국에 제공하지 않고 있다. 한국 위성이 수적으로 증가했다고는 하지만, 경험을 토대로 한 분석기술은 미국이 앞서는게 현실이다.   미국의 ‘초고속·초정밀 경보 및 정찰’이 한반도 감시망을 완성하는 ‘천조국 위력’이고, 한·미 정보 협력이 대북 대비의 핵심이라는 현실을 다 알고있는데, 기밀을 유출한 그 사람만 모르는듯 싶다.   상응 조치로 미국과의 정보 공유를 끊는다는 발상 자체가  국가안보 의식이 결여된 종북적인 좌편향된 반국가세력임을 스스로 입증한 셈이다.

🚨 Threat intelligence spotlight: NCSC Warns of Ai-Driven “Patch Wave”  The UK’s National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) has issued a warning to organisations to prepare for an Ai-driven “patch wave”.  As Ai tools rapidly identify long-standing vulnerabilities across systems, organisations are facing a surge in required software updates, and a shrinking window between disclosure and exploitation.  What used to take days, can now take minutes.  The NCSC has described this as a “forced correction” of accumulated technical debt. This isn’t about more patches, it’s a shift in how risk builds: 🔵 Vulnerabilities are found faster. 🔵 Exploitation happens sooner. 🔵 Backlogs become a real exposure.  For many organisations, the challenge isn’t visibility. It’s keeping up.  What organisations should consider: ✔️ Prioritise internet-facing systems first. ✔️ Enable automatic or rapid patching where possible. ✔️ Review and reduce legacy or unsupported systems. ✔️ Focus on patch cadence, not just coverage.  For SMEs in particular, this is a clear signal that patching needs to become more structured and consistent.  This is not a future problem, it’s already happening. sample_tweets.text: ベトナム関連APTと疑われるOceanLotus（APT32）が、Pythonパッケージの公開リポジトリである「PyPI」へ悪性パッケージを投入し、これまで未確認だったマルウェアファミリ「ZiChatBot」を配布していた可能性が報告されました。注目点は、業務用チャットSaaS「Zulip」のクラウドAPIをC2チャネルとして用いていた点と、無害に見えるパッケージが裏で悪性パッケージを依存関係として取り込む二層構成によって、悪性コンポーネントの発見を遅らせる設計が確認された点です。  具体的には、利用者が直接インストールする側のパッケージ自体には悪性コードを含めず、そのパッケージが内部で別の悪性パッケージを「依存ライブラリ」として自動的に呼び込む構造になっており、利用者が手にとるパッケージのソースを目視確認しても異常が見つかりにくい設計となっています。  公開パッケージリポジトリを悪用するサプライチェーン攻撃は近年活発化しており、本件は国家関与が疑われるアクターによる事例の追加として位置づけられます。  【要点の整理】  ・PyPIに投入された配布形式（whlファイル、Python向けパッケージ形式）から発見されたもの  ・攻撃者は3つのPyPIプロジェクトを作成しており、その一つである表向き正規に見える「termncolor」が裏で悪性の「colorinal」に依存する構成  ・実体は「colorinal」内に同梱された terminate[.]dll／terminate[.]so で、Windows／Linux双方を対象としていた  ・C2にはZulipのクラウド版（helper[.]zulipchat[.]com）のREST APIが用いられ、ハートの絵文字でリアクションを返す通信仕様  ・正規SaaSドメインへのHTTPS通信に紛れるため、ネットワーク層での検知回避に寄与  ・サイドローディングには正規実行ファイル vcpktsvr[.]exe が悪用  ・帰属判定はベンダー独自のコード類似度解析ツールにより、既知OceanLotus関連サンプルと約64％の類似と評価され、断定ではなく「疑い」との位置づけ  ・該当パッケージはベンダー通報後にPyPIから削除済みで、現時点でテレメトリおよび公開報告のいずれにおいても感染は未観測  OceanLotusはアジア太平洋を主な標的としつつ中東への活動拡大傾向が指摘されてきており、世界中のPython利用者を対象とし得るPyPI経由配送は、同グループによる攻撃対象の地理的・領域的な拡大傾向の延長線上にある動きとも読み取れます。  詳細は以下を参照： securelist.com/oceanlotus-su…

On-chain RWA Data Analysis & Tokenization Metrics (May 7, 2026)  RWA tokenization remains in a steady expansion phase, with Distributed Asset Value holding at $31.12B (+3.52% 30d) and Represented Asset Value at $391.95B (+2.02% 30d), while total asset holders reached 748,954 (+3.45%). Over the past 24 hours, market-wide TVL has remained stable, though select assets like BUIDL (+1.98%) and certain Active Strategies (+10%+) showed positive momentum. This reflects a structurally resilient expansion, anchored in Treasuries and high-confidence instruments, with potential disruption if redemption velocity exceeded new issuance by 1.5%, which could redistribute liquidity across mid-tier assets.  The market structure remains concentrated but exhibits targeted activity in commodity-linked RWAs. US Treasuries dominate with USYC $2.9B, BUIDL $2.6B, and USDY $2.1B, while newly issued Asset-Backed Credit and real estate tranches have expanded the pipeline. Commodity RWAs such as XAUT ($2.6B) and PAXG ($2.1B) recorded 24-hour trading volumes surpassing BTC on some platforms, signaling that real-world assets are now actively programmable and tradable on-chain. This illustrates that tokenization is enabling liquidity for previously “static” physical assets, bridging TradFi certainty with blockchain immediacy.  Capital flows indicate concentrated institutional participation, with DeFi integration progressing but still facing friction. Active Mcap is approximately $26.67B, Onchain Mcap $28B, and DeFi Active TVL around $2.01B. Protocols like Aave, Pendle, and Ondo facilitate collateralized lending, tradable RWA derivatives, and tokenized stocks, yet recent DeFi credit shocks (e.g., Kelp DAO bridge incidents) highlight the vulnerability of distributed collateral. The resulting divergence between Represented and Distributed Asset Value underscores that tokenization is only partially realized until on-chain cash flows and settlement reliability mature.  Monitoring issuer-level inflows/outflows and the ratio of distributed to represented value is critical. Metrics such as commodity RWA volumes, Treasury stability, and newly tokenized credit tranches reveal system sensitivity: high trading activity in commodities demonstrates liquidity potential, while gaps between represented and distributed assets reflect trust and verification bottlenecks.  Historically, RWA adoption phases show concentration in low-risk Treasuries before expansion into commodities, credit, and alternative assets. The current focus on commodity RWAs, tokenized real estate, and private credit illustrates the structural transition toward functional diversification. However, privacy and settlement remain the primary bottlenecks: institutions require confidential transaction capabilities and verifiable counterparty acceptance, which are not yet universally implemented. Addressing these constraints will unlock the full potential of tokenized assets.  Two structural paths exist. Continued inflows into Treasuries and high-confidence commodities would sustain steady growth while preserving concentration. Alternatively, if redemption pressures or counterparty hesitancy increase, liquidity may temporarily consolidate or shift to alternative protocols and chains. Both scenarios remain conditional, reflecting the pace at which tokenization infrastructure, DeFi integration, and privacy-preserving settlement converge. The broader trend indicates RWA tokenization is transitioning from experimental deployment to a credible, institutionally integrated on-chain financial layer.  #RWA #OnChainRWA #TokenizedTreasuries #RealYield #InstitutionalFlows #XAUT #PAXG

<1. So what's a Network Interface Card (NIC)?  If you have ever taken time to think about how data is transferred from one computer to another, even within our homes, you might have imagined something simple, maybe like a USB port where electrical signals somehow pass through the port and straight to applications. In the case of Wi-Fi, you might imagine something similar, except this time the physical medium is radio waves instead of wires.  What actually happens is far more complicated than that, and we want to talk about part of it, mostly through the lens of electrical signaling.  The NIC is not just an Ethernet port. In my opinion, it is a computer living inside your computer, but specialized for networking. It is a highly specialized piece of hardware responsible for processing packets, moving data between memory and the network, handling electrical signaling, filtering traffic, and communicating with the Operating System. A lot of computation happens before applications even get to send or receive data across the network.  The NIC chip is broadly made up of two major components: the PHY and the MAC. What I'm mostly concerned about as a driver writer is the MAC (Media Access Control).  The PHY (Physical Layer Device), as you might have guessed, handles the electrical signaling side of things. It converts electrical signals from the Ethernet cable into digital data the computer can process, and also converts outgoing digital data into electrical signals suitable for transmission across the wire. This side of networking involves a lot of Digital Signal Processing (DSP), including things like noise cancellation, signal recovery, filtering, and encoding.  Device drivers are mostly writing code to efficiently move packets up the networking stack. By networking stack, I mean layers like Ethernet → ARP/IP → TCP/UDP → HTTP. But the basic idea is to simply move packets efficiently between the NIC and memory.  The diagram basically shows how packets move inside the Network Interface Card (NIC), from the computer’s memory all the way down to electrical signals on the Ethernet cable.   You can mentally split it into two halves. The upper half is mostly the computer systems side, this is where things like the PCI (Peripheral Component Interconnect) interface, DMA (Direct Memory Access) engine, descriptor management, packet buffers, MMIO (Memory-Mapped Input/Output) register access, and the MAC (Media Access Control) live. This side is responsible for moving packets between system memory and the NIC, processing Ethernet frames, filtering traffic, generating interrupts, and communicating with the Operating System driver.      The lower half is mostly the PHY (Physical Layer Device) side, where the NIC deals with actual electrical signaling. This part contains Digital Signal Processing (DSP) hardware responsible for encoding signals, recovering timing information, cancelling noise and crosstalk, converting between analog electrical waveforms and digital data, and ultimately driving signals onto the Ethernet cable.   At a high level, the packet flow looks something like this:  System Memory -> DMA Engine -> MAC -> PHY -> Ethernet Cable  The most important thing to understand from the diagram is that modern nic is both a packet-processing engine and a high-speed signal-processing device working together to move data efficiently between the network and the Operating System.  Lots of new words for people who are new to this side of things, but most of the terms mean almost exactly what they semantically suggest. As we go forward, I will try to explain what each one means and the implications they have for driver code

The company is proving it can command better terms on legacy business. • 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐮𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝐎𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐕𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞 — The company remains the only commercial manufacturer aligned with the FDA's proposed low-nicotine standard. If enacted, 22nd Century holds the keys to the compliance kingdom via licensing agreements.  🐻 𝐁𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐞 • 𝐂𝐚𝐭𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐩𝐡𝐢𝐜 𝐕𝐋𝐍 𝐃𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐒𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐥 — Booking negative $3K in VLN revenue due to returns just one quarter after large initial stocking orders is a massive red flag. It suggests that while retailers put the product on shelves, consumers are simply not buying it. • 𝐑𝐮𝐧𝐰𝐚𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐃𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐥𝐲 𝐒𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐭 — Operating expenses reversed their downward trend, increasing to $2.4M. With a quarterly EBITDA burn of $2.6M and only $9.5M in the bank, the company faces severe dilution risks within the next 12 months.  ⚖️ 𝐕𝐞𝐫𝐝𝐢𝐜𝐭: 🔴🔴 Highly Bearish. When a company sacrifices its legacy revenue base to pivot to a high-margin proprietary product, that proprietary product must sell. A quarter with zero VLN revenue completely invalidates the current growth thesis.  𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬  🔴🔴 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐕𝐋𝐍 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐥𝐥𝐚𝐩𝐬𝐞 [NEW] The most alarming data point in this report is the total lack of VLN sales. After reporting ~8,800 cartons shipped in Q4 2025 (generating $129K), Q1 2026 net revenues for the VLN product line plummeted to negative $3,000. Management blamed 'customer returns and product exchanges to the new VLN branding.' This directly contradicts previous management commentary about growing consumer adoption. If the product lacks end-consumer sell-through, the entire turnaround thesis fails.  🔴 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐟𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐓𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐮𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐃𝐫𝐢𝐟𝐭 In early 2025, management promised EBITDA breakeven by late 2025. That target drifted to 'first half of 2026', and in this report, the language softened further to 'advancing toward EBITDA breakeven.' The numbers show why: Operating expenses reversed their downward trajectory, climbing to $2.4M from $2.0M in Q4. Operating loss widened to $3.0M. The cost structure is inflating before the new revenues arrive.  🔴 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐡 𝐑𝐮𝐧𝐰𝐚𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐔𝐧𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐲 𝐓𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭 [NEW] The company ended Q1 with $9.5M in cash and cash equivalents, up from $7.1M at year-end (largely reflecting working capital movements and previous non-dilutive settlements). However, with an Adjusted EBITDA burn rate of $2.6M per quarter and mounting inventory ($4.3M), the company has less than a year of functional runway. Given the lack of debt, future capital needs will almost certainly require highly dilutive equity raises.  ⚪ 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐜𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐲 𝐆𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐬 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐅𝐢𝐥𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐂𝐢𝐠𝐚𝐫𝐬 [NEW] The one bright spot operationally was the success of contract renegotiations. Filtered cigar net revenues jumped to $0.87M, up sequentially from $0.4M in Q4 2025. This reflects management's successful implementation of higher pricing on legacy contracts. Contract manufacturing cigarettes also rebounded sequentially to $2.8M from $2.6M. These segments are providing essential life support while the branded strategy stalls.  🟢 𝐑𝐞𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐥 𝐅𝐨𝐨𝐭𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝟏𝟎𝟎𝐦𝐦 𝐈𝐧𝐧𝐨𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 The company is pushing hard on physical availability, targeting over 5,000 retail outlets by the end of 2026. To support this, they are innovating on product form factors, explicitly completing product prototyping for a 100mm format VLN cigarette ahead of a planned PMTA authorization. This matches consumer preferences in key demographics and expands the addressable market beyond the standard king-size format.  🟢 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐋𝐢𝐜𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐌𝐚𝐜𝐫𝐨 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐮𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝐂𝐚𝐥𝐥 𝐎𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 Management continues to position the company as a toll booth for the FDA's proposed low-nicotine mandate. Their technology road map focuses on creating a flexible platform that spans multiple categories (pouches, moist snuff, filtered cigars) using proprietary low-nicotine tobacco. Their explicit goal is to make all authorized combustible products available for licensing, providing Big Tobacco with ready-to-market compliance pathways if regulations tighten.  𝐎𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐊𝐏𝐈𝐬  𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐮𝐟𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐂𝐢𝐠𝐚𝐫𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐬 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞 (𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟏): $2.85 million Reversing. After bottoming out at $2.6M in Q4 2025, this segment ticked up sequentially. However, it remains down 43% YoY from $5.0M in Q1 2025, reflecting the deliberate exit from high-volume, low-margin export customers.  𝐀𝐝𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐄𝐁𝐈𝐓𝐃𝐀 (𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟏): $(2.60) million Stable. The company is stuck in a tight band of unprofitability. Despite the sequential bump in revenue, Adjusted EBITDA worsened slightly from $(2.4)M in Q4 2025 and is roughly flat YoY compared to $(2.3)M in Q1 2025.  𝐆𝐮𝐢𝐝𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞  𝐑𝐞𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐥 𝐒𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐓𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐞𝐭: > 5,000 outlets Accelerating. The company expects to scale distribution from its current footprint (approximately 2,000 stores) to over 5,000 retail outlets by the end of 2026, targeting all classes of trade.  𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐟𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐓𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐞𝐭: Advancing toward breakeven Decelerating. Management has entirely removed explicit timeline targets for EBITDA breakeven (previously guided for H1 2026). The focus has shifted to generic statements about achieving profitability 'as higher-margin revenues scale.'  𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐐𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬  𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐕𝐋𝐍 𝐃𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐕𝐨𝐢𝐝 VLN net revenues were zero this quarter due to customer returns. What is the actual retail sell-through rate for the 8,800 cartons shipped in Q4, and why should investors believe the consumer wants this product?  𝐁𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧 𝐆𝐨𝐚𝐥𝐩𝐨𝐬𝐭𝐬 The explicit timeline for EBITDA breakeven appears to have been removed from the prepared remarks. What is the new internal target date, and what quarterly volume of VLN sales is required to achieve it?  𝐂𝐚𝐩𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐍𝐞𝐞𝐝𝐬 With $9.5M in cash, rising operating expenses, and an ongoing $2.6M quarterly EBITDA burn, how does management plan to fund the expansion to 5,000 stores without a highly dilutive equity raise in the second half of 2026?

sample_tweets.text: ‼️🇪🇨 CACPE Pastaza allegedly breached exposing 18 million Ecuadorian civil registry records via an unprotected API  A threat actor claims that the identity validation API of CACPE Pastaza, an Ecuadorian cooperative, was pwned through an unprotected proxy endpoint that queries the national civil registry, exposing over 18 million records updated as of today. The post is being released for free and includes a working cURL exploitation example showing how anyone can pull complete personal records by submitting a national ID number.  Post details:  ▸ Actor(s): GondorPe ▸ Sector: Finance / Government (civil registry via cooperative API) ▸ Type: Data Leak / API Exposure ▸ Price: Free ▸ Records: 18,000,000+ ▸ Country: Ecuador ▸ Date: 06/05/2026  Compromised data:  ▪ Full names (apellidos, nombres) ▪ National ID number (NUI / cédula) ▪ Date of birth ▪ Place of birth ▪ Home address (domicilio, calle, número) ▪ Marital status ▪ Gender ▪ Nationality ▪ Father's and mother's names ▪ Profession ▪ Date of ID issuance ▪ Citizenship status

sample_tweets.text: EXCLUSIVE INTERVIEW: Z-Pentest Daily Dark Web conducted an interview with the group known as “Z-Pentest” regarding intrusion operations, security failures, operational security, and modern cyber conflict.  ⸻  1. You present yourselves as a penetration testing group. How do you differentiate between legitimate security research and unauthorized intrusion?  Z-Pentest: “Legitimate research always starts with permission. Without it, it is already intrusion, and no ‘oops, I was just testing’ will save you. Unauthorized intrusion is usually accompanied by blackmail attempts, attacks on critical systems, or public data leaks. A legitimate tester follows agreed rules, transparent processes, and authorization. The difference is similar to a doctor performing surgery versus someone attempting it without permission.”  ⸻  2. Can you walk us through a recent operation from initial access to data exfiltration? What was the exact attack chain?  Z-Pentest: “The operation followed a classic approach. Initial access was achieved through phishing and exploitation of known vulnerabilities. Once inside, the objective was to remain unnoticed while collecting data and maintaining operational stealth until the operation was completed.”  ⸻  3. Many groups claim breaches without solid proof. How do you verify and demonstrate that your claims are real?  Z-Pentest: “We maintain logs and operational evidence related to intrusions. In many situations, organizations publicly acknowledge incidents much later, sometimes months or even years after the initial compromise.”  ⸻  4. What are the most common security misconfigurations or weaknesses you consistently exploit across targets?  Z-Pentest: “We will not disclose operational details, but weak security practices and misconfigurations remain common across many organizations.”  ⸻  5. Do you rely more on automation, tools, scripts, AI, or is your work still heavily manual and human-driven?  Z-Pentest: “Most operations remain heavily manual. Automation, scripts, and AI can assist, but human experience and decision-making still play the primary role.”  ⸻  6. What is your typical initial access vector: phishing, credential reuse, exploiting vulnerabilities, or something else?  Z-Pentest: “We use a combination of methods including phishing, exploitation of vulnerabilities, credential reuse, and other techniques depending on the target environment.”  ⸻  7. Have you observed defenders improving, or are organizations still making the same fundamental mistakes?  Z-Pentest: “Many organizations invest in security programs, but fundamental mistakes still remain common. In some cases, security processes appear more focused on compliance than actual protection.”  ⸻  8. Some groups exaggerate or recycle old data to stay relevant. How do you respond to skepticism about the authenticity of your leaks?  Z-Pentest: “Organizations and governments rarely acknowledge incidents immediately. Public explanations often reference technical failures or system issues before the full reality becomes known later.”  ⸻  9. Do you consider your activities ethical in any sense, or purely opportunistic?  Z-Pentest: “We view our activities as more than financial motivation. From our perspective, cyber operations are part of a broader information conflict environment.”  ⸻  10. What role does geopolitics play in your targeting decisions, if any?  Z-Pentest: “Geopolitical considerations influence operational priorities and targeting decisions.”  ⸻  11. Have you ever aborted an operation after gaining access? If yes, what made you stop?  Z-Pentest: “There have been situations where operations had to pause temporarily due to unexpected activity from system operators inside the environment.”  ⸻  12. What is the most technically challenging environment you have encountered, and why?  Z-Pentest: “Complex systems exist, but so far operations have remained manageable from a technical perspective.”  ⸻ 👇

sample_tweets.text: Boris cannot be stopped

I had a few people tag me saying this is novel malware.  This is not novel malware.  This is like ... malware spearphishing, or something, I don't know a good way to describe it. Regardless, this isn't new.  This malware idea of luring in nerds and using mods to deploy malware has been a thing for awhile. If my memory serves me correctly (it usually doesn't) it got pretty popular around COVID, but it's been around since before even that.  This particular method of delivering malware is tricky. If someone shares a malicious mod on a mod sharing website it usually infects a low number of people and is flagged pretty quickly. The meta quickly shifted to people using malicious mods to target high-profile people. Most notably, cryptodraining nerds.  This also kind of sucks though because it requires interacting with the target and trying to gain their trust. For serious malware campaigns this method will not suffice, you won't get many infected machines. However, it will suffice for specific targets.  Anyway, I hate to bring bad news, but the likelihood of this being prosecuted is basically zero. Cybercrime is rampant, it's nonstop and around the clock, and serious Threat Actors are ransoming critical infrastructure and delivering malware on a massive scale.  Law enforcement having to cooperate with multiple countries about a malicious ... Minecraft mod ... is not a thing. The exception to this is if by chance this person happened to successfully steal $10,000,000+ from these malicious Minecraft mods. This would get this a substantial amount of attention. Fortunately (or unfortunately?) based on this write-up this person provided I do not believe this is the case.  This whole malicious Minecraft mod thingie has been a thing for awhile though. Google these thingies for more information:  - Stargazers Ghost Network (Minecraft) - Fractureiser

The initial proof-of-concept was released in C-sharp.  Using this method to dump credentials is iffy because it requires administrative access and some security access tokens which can raise some flags.  First, Edge is Chromium based. This is a Chromium thing but (if my memory serves me correctly) a unique attribute to Edge exclusively. However, because it is Chromium based this may impact other Chromium bases. It requires more investigation. Edge is a primary target because it's the default Windows browser and used in enterprise environments.  Secondly, as far as malware goes, this is yet another method to potentially dump credentials on a home users machine. There are a few different ways. This method doesn't surprise me. However, successfully using this method is an enterprise environment would be difficult to use. It would require administrative access and some security access tokens which would immediately raise some flags.  In other words, this method is interesting, I like the research performed, however it isn't something super super critical. If you're using this method in an enterprise environment then that company has been completely compromised down to the bone and they've got much larger issues.  The code and research is really cool though. I just wish it wasn't written in C-sharp (I have an irrational disdain to .NET, especially lately).

I've got a really silly idea for malware.  Windows 11 now have Windows.Graphics from the Windows Runtime API.  You can use it for taking screenshots. It's supposed to be better than the native WINAPI method because something about GPU rendering stuff, I don't know, I can't remember.  Anyway  Windows 11 also ships with an OCR library from the Windows-something-something in the WinRT as part of their AI stuff.  The point being: I think I can take a fancy screenshot of an application, like Slack, Microsoft Teams, or Discord, using WinRT then use WinRT to OCR it into readable and parseable text from C/C++  It is basically a really convoluted way to do keylogging or espionage, or whatever.  For extra flavor, use WinRT to upload the OCRd text to a remote host. Why do this instead of WinHTTP or Windows Sockets? Literally no reason other than curiosity. I have no idea how this would appear under the scope of an EDR.  Sometimes you need to try silly things.

You buy a German anvil. It contains 83 moving parts and requires winding twice a day. It's forged from excellent steel, holds tolerances across all three striking faces to within three microns, includes a beautifully indexed horn-adjustment mechanism nobody asked for, and requires a proprietary 11-point spanner should you need to replace the rebound calibration bushing. It runs flawlessly for years, but one day it starts up in limp mode because the onboard anvil-management system detects that it's overdue for its 50,000-strike inspection.  You search AliExpress for a Chinese anvil, and are presented with a multitude of offerings from such household-name brands as DUKXJYIBF, HDBTGMXI, AND UEJQIP. They're all priced to within a few pennies of each other, appear completely identical except for the nameplate, and obviously all came out of the same factory. You text your blacksmith friend to ask if they're legit. He tells you he got one like that from KIXJBU a few years ago, and that it's been great and a terrific deal. You thank him, but KIXJBU seems to have folded so you buy the one from UEJQIP. When it arrives, it feels suspiciously light. You scratch it and realize it's iron-plated aluminum.  You buy an American anvil. It's five times the price of the competition, but it comes from a brand that your great-grandfather used to love. It comes boxed with a warranty registration postcard, twenty pages of safety instructions, assay certificate, and a regulatory slip which lists its FCC certification and ITAR registration. It looks just like your friend's KIXJBU. There's a "Made In China" sticker on the bottom.  You buy a Russian anvil. It arrives coated in cosmoline, wrapped in newspaper from 1974, and weighing 40% more than advertised. The finish looks like it was machined with a shovel. The face is not flat, but somehow this does not matter. You drop it off a truck, accidentally leave it outside for six winters, and use it to straighten a bulldozer blade. It's fine.  You buy a Swedish anvil. It comes flat-packed in a long cardboard box with cheerful Neo-Grotesk lettering and a line drawing of a smiling man assembling it with an Allen key. The instructions contain no words, only pictograms showing the anvil face, horn, waist, feet, and 112 identical-looking fasteners. Halfway through assembly, you discover that the pritchel hole was installed upside down, but only because you used peg B17 where you should have used peg B71. Once assembled, it is clean, stable, and works better than it has any right to. You immediately wonder whether you should have bought two.  You buy a Japanese anvil. It arrives wrapped in rice paper inside a paulownia box, accompanied by a certificate bearing three generations of signatures and a photograph of the first production example being presented to the Emperor. The face has been hand-polished by a seventy-eight-year-old master whose family has made striking surfaces since the Muromachi period. You are given detailed instructions for oiling it with a cloth folded in a specific way. It is the most beautiful object you own. You never quite work up the nerve to strike it.
